speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't ever remember having been there to hear him.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The men talked about him. I can't remember now what he preached about or anything. But I know he'd give a sermon.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: This is Dick Farrell. Dick Farrell, yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you call him the pack sack, pack sack Dick?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, pack sack Dick. He'd just travel around all over the pack sack on the back. That's what he did. He just made the camp take up the collection. That's the way he lived. I don't know if he's a real preacher or if he was just making the easy living. I was trying to think.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was trying to think that he didn't sell jewelry at the same time. There was one man come around I know selling jewelry. Oh, he'd have all kinds of jewelry and watches and everything into the camps. But I don't think it was Dick. It was another one. We had all them characters around regular.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you ever listen to any of the sermons?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. But I can't remember what they were. Hellfire and brimstone, I guess. Yeah, that's what he did.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They'd have the funky's in. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And the man. But if he was around when we lived up, lived in camp, I don't remember ever inviting him. Yeah, we used to come around to Camp 7. It was Camp 7 south of the Bovill.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He'd come in there and I'd say, I think you and Ed was living there at the time. But you maybe didn't know about it. If we ever went, I don't remember having been there. That's where I started out, was in Camp 7.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: In the camp.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you work in the camp?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. Ed was working and they had a cabin there they were living in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can't just remember for sure what time of the evening we went.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's right.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Had the... They had just these little shacks that we lived in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. Up there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then we carried water from the river creek.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was it? Was it a reef or a river?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was a creek.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Carried that in and heated water to wash in on the board. A little pioneer stopper.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This was in the 20th?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was 27th. 27th. 27th. Fall of 27th and 28th.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Exactly where was Camp 7 located?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was about four miles south of Bovill. It was off of the Elk River Road. It was west of the Elk River Road. Like a railroad running into it. A railroad come in from... Or did the railroad come straight through from Bovill down and then went down down to... I was trying to picture where the railroad track was. Well, it went down.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Wasn't that Boulder Creek?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I've forgotten that it was down there. Then went clear back in around to... Oh, way south there to Camp 31 and that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You know, it crossed the highway there. We turned in and crossed... Drove down... Crossed that field down into the camp before we crossed the railroad track. But where was the railroad track at the camp, I can't recall.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was it near our shack?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It come out from Helmer. Come out from Helmer. Then it come up to us and then it went on through down to... I forget what they called the place down there. I remember I was walking on the railroad. It was about 14 miles south.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You down by Park? By Park?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, down through there. Green Park and Elk River, over in that area. I can't remember their names anymore. I'll get through there. Then I remember somebody talks about it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember any of the practical jokes that the Lumberjacks are supposed to be famous for?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They were short sheeting them. Then one, they'd block their sleeves and a coat. They'd put one sleeve in the side of the other. Then they'd put a block in there. Then they'd pull it tight. I'll tell you that was... Then they'd roll the blankets. You'd take a blanket and roll a blanket and you couldn't find no hand to start unrolling it. Then you'd just take an ordinary blanket and roll it up. That would all be tucked in there and you could not find the place to start. If you didn't know how to do it, you couldn't find the place to start. That was a mess, that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know one night I had to go to Moscow to a doctor. I had the appointment at 7 o 'clock and I left. When I got back to camp, I heard Jimmy Sago from Boville, and I forget who the other kid was. They were sleeping in the bunker. One was in the bunker above me. He was just snacking and snacking. I didn't have no light or anything. I knew just about what had happened. So instead of getting down and playing the sheets, I just lifted the blankets and slept on top of the sheet. I made a short sheet in my bed. Fixed that so you'd go in and you couldn't get your feet only about halfway down. And when they'd done that right, that was quite a job. I didn't say anything. I just went to sleep. Never said a word in the morning. And Jimmy and... I can't think of the other kids. They were just kids, about 18.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: They said, what does it matter, mother?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You couldn't get to bed last night. I said, you didn't have any trouble. And they were snacking and didn't say anything. And they were always late to get enough. They wouldn't get up until the breakfast bell rang. Well, they had their cork boots there. And the shoelaces were about that long. And I got them and I started and I just tied knots. But both shoes, both pair. There was just nothing but a string of knots about that long for a dot dot. Well, they'd get up just in time for breakfast and then they'd come out. And they'd sit around and when the truck was ready to leave, they'd go pull their boots on. The truck was already late to take us to work. They were not made to dive for their boots. They couldn't get them on. You'd see them two boys cry. They could carry their boots and tie them knots as they went out. Well, that was done quite often. You'd catch any late sleeper and then he'd miss the... He'd either have to run with his boots carrying them out and tie the knots. He couldn't get them on, you know, he'd pull them tied them out. That was a common stunt. Of course, they knew who did it. They knew right off. But they never pulled no more stunts than they do. That was the end of it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That sounds pretty mild.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that's mild. There were some rough ones.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But I can't remember all of them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Let me ask a question of Helena while you think. I was wondering how many other women were up there. Well, let's see. There was Maddie.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was the name of that other one with Maddie?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was only about four of us at the time. I think it was four. I think so.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You were just all wise? How were you working?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Mm -hmm. Just wise. Well, there was two or three flunkies, so women flunkies. Yeah, the flunkies, too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now, what's a flunky do?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It's a waitress. Oh, a flunkie. Flunkies. They always called them flunkies. One may have hired him, they hired him as flunkies.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can't remember the one that was with Maddie so much. I can't recall her name. I haven't been able to think of her name yet. I don't think about it. Well, I can't remember if there was more than three of us in there. That came.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Almost seems like there was just the three and then the two flunkies. I was thinking there were four. It might have only been three.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you think of any others?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Anyway, when they hooked up a team to sled and take us into town, I know. I remember going. They had a woman, a druggist in Bowville at the time. We went in there. It was cold. They'd take us in for shopping. Oh. They were pretty good to us there. I like that camp. We went and took our washing down to their washroom.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that the place where we got the bear?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They had killed a bear just out. They had come around to the junk cayons. They had killed a bear. They gave each of us a chunk of bear meat. Ed had always made the threat that no one would ever feed him bear meat. I know how I worked real hard to camouflage that bear meat. I got him to eat bear meat. I seasoned it up and put dressing with it and got it all fixed up and tasted it. I thought it tasted pretty good. I gave him some bear meat to eat. That was good.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He said, what kind is it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I said, well, somebody, I guess it was a deer or something. They killed it and they gave us some from the camp. I said, it's sure dark. I said, yeah, it is dark. It's getting mead all dark like that. He ate it and took a second helping. Of course, me and my big mouth, I couldn't keep this any longer. I said, that's bear meat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was the end of the beat, too. I ate some more. The fellas I've got it was Oscar Thurgoods. The guy that got it was some relation of Oscar Sturgoods from West Virginia. He just couldn't wait until he got some of that bear meat. We're going to have bear meat for dinner. I walked in and I said, you can have your bear meat. I don't want it. I walked in for dinner and we had great big steaks. It wasn't a very big camp at that time. We had great big steaks, you know, and I took one. I ate it and boy, that's fine bear meat. I didn't say anything. We got out and they said, oh, I thought you didn't like bear meat. I said, I don't. The police said, you ate that. I said, that wasn't bear meat. Oh, yes, it was. The next day the same way. I ate a lot of meat. I went out and they were raving about it. The third day we went in. The minute I stepped in that dining room, I knew what we had, because I can smell bear meat cooking. And they grabbed a big steak and handed me the platter. I said, no, thanks. Don't be about care for any of you, Daddy. I went ahead and ate. They never said a word. They was eating and they'd look at me and they'd eat some more.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When I got out back to the bunkhouse, I said, well, how'd you like the bear meat? They said, was that bear meat?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I said, yes, the other was good steak, but today it was bear meat. It wasn't as good as I thought it to be. They didn't like it either. Well, bear meat is too sweet. I don't like it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Sweet?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Sweet, oh, yeah. And then I've never liked it. It might be partly the reason that when old John Pickard shot a bear over here in the park, naturalist old 1914 -15 in there somewhere.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This would be Deathy Park?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. Maybe a little later than that. And I think it was the old man Pickard. And he had an undertaken prior, right where the Standard Oil Station is down here. And in the front, he had a big, I think it was, I forget if he sold buggies or something, but it was a great big building there, old plate glass windows, you know, and the undertaking part of this was on the back. Well, he dressed the bear out and he hung it up there in the front in the window. And it looked just like a person hanging there. You see a bear dressed out and they looked like a person. It hung there and the train used to pull in here about 8 o 'clock at night.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you hear about it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. There was two or three women from Troy come off of that train. They come walking up there, looked at that, just right over in a dead tent, you passed right out there, but he thought he had a butchered man hanging there in the window. Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That might be part of the reason too that I don't like bear meat, but I've never been able to. That's why Ed said he would never eat it. He said when it's butchered it hangs there. It looks just like a man, just like a person. Exactly. He had it there right out in the front end, undertaking prior to his end of the bank. Oh, geez. I'll tell you, Lata would just break anybody of eating bear meat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It's greasy too. He was pretty proud of it. If it's cold, that's even worse. It's just greasy. Terrible. They don't need to feed me any better either.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you eat a lot of wild game up at the camp?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. We never got wild. That is the only time we ever got any wild game. If we drove fishing, if we could talk the cook into cooking them for us, we had a lot of fish in there. Right there is where Ed really took the fishing. He used to go up here, but he could never catch anything out in the creek there. When he got up there, and he got pretty lucky getting fish, and from then on he wanted to fish all the time. But before that, he never cared for it. That was a good creek there. Oh, yeah. There were a lot of fish in that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, Mrs. ...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was Sturgill the boss in that one? She wasn't in there then, though, was she? Who?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Was Mrs.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Sturgill there then?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was trying to think. It seemed to me like she was there for a while.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know. She was in ...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, did they go on to Camp 1 too? Was she there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: If not, she was in that camp too, because I remember she was so allergic to everything. She kept herself in the cabin, and it was all just steamy all the time, because if she got out dry, she would just ...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, she was there for a while. I was going to ask her a little bit ago if she wasn't there. I thought, well, maybe I was mistaken. But I know she was in that camp for a while. Yeah, I remember being ... I was trying to think if that was the camp, and if he was the boss, and she was there. Yeah, he was the boss. He opened the camp up in the fall and started it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was Welch at Camp 1.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was Ed Welch.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Ed Welch, yeah. Little Hiram Cole was the clerk there. Hiram Cole was the clerk for Oscar's Church.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Margaret Smith's uncle.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He was a little bit of a man. A little bit of a fellow. He'd heard himself, fell off of a roof or something, when a young fellow never did grow. His back was bothered. But he was sure a nice man. He'd do anything in the world for you. But if you want to know, this wasn't a trick. This happened up in Camp 35, and that was along 1940. It was late. They were shutting the camp down the next day before Christmas. And two fellows from Bowville decided they were going to fill their bunkhouse. We all went to town into Clarky, got loaded up, come home. I went to bed. Most of us did, but Bill Zimmerman and Leroy Harris, there was about four fellows decided they were going to fill their bunkhouse that night. Everybody had been leaving. Well, they'd go and they'd carry, go down to one bunkhouse and get some fellows. They'd come carrying them through the one I was sleeping in. I was right next to them. They were figuring who they could get next. Well, one fellow had locked the bunkhouse door. They took an axe and shot the door down. It went in. And they were carrying that fellow up there, took the mattress and all. Then as he went by my bunk, Bill Zimmerman, he happened to think about me. And they said, you're next. No, I'm not. I'm not going. You're not taking me in there. Oh, yes.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What were they doing?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They were going to fill the bunkhouse up. There was only two of them in his room for 10. And they were just going to have a full bunkhouse the last night. And they had three or four different fellows in there already. And they come back after me and they just grabbed the mattress, the whole work, the man right in it, to carry them in and put them on the bed.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And when Bill got out of bed in the morning, he never made his bed. He just threw the covers back and there was a big, deep hole right in the bed. So they come in there and I said, well, just wait. I said, you don't have to carry my bed in. I said, just my blankets. I said, I'll walk in. Okay. I got a dress. They grabbed my stuff, carried it in, wind up, and I made my bed up at the other end. They had it all nice. And when they got done with it, I said, well, you boy is going to carry it back. They turned around here. I was standing with a full bucket of water over Bill's bed. Bill looked, hey, Lira, Lira. He said, he's going to dump water in my bed. He said, no, he won't do it. He said, I don't know.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And I said, what are you going to carry back now? I said, do you get this water?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I'm too arguing. Leroy says, tell him to go to hell. Bill turned around and says, go to hell. Whiting the water with him. And you're laying there. There was some off that beach. Oh, gee. Oh, Bill, he just sat down and cried. So then, no more blankets, you know. We each got so many blankets. And it was cold. It was just before Christmas. So then he would go to sleep with Leroy, two of them, and just a little bed. Well, then about 1 .30 in the morning, they went out and they rang the breakfast bell to get up. You know, it was a triangle and they rang it. Some of them old timers there, they never thought they got up and dressed. They sat around, sat around. Well, I had a long time till breakfast.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, after the heat was a mess. I don't know. I only had a bunkhouse can. The boss went through all day and had a mess in that bunkhouse. The boss canned everybody that was in there, but there was a couple of them. It wasn't their fault. It was explained to them, the boss left. But they let me go back to my own bed again. They went right back and made my bed.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, after one bucket of water.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: That's all they needed, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that was sure a mess that night.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But it was funny, them old fellas, they were up and all dressed. Well, and some of them, we used to put up... No, we didn't take lunch that morning, I was going to say. They used to make their lunch right off, but then we'd all leave in camp, so they didn't have to put up the lunch. I thought it was a long wait from 1 .30 to 5 .30 to 2 to get up now.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, how did the ladies occupy themselves? How did you occupy yourself in the camp?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I can't just remember so much of that one, but it was just fun just being in there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I guess, was that the one where Ed set up a tetherball, and they came around and they played tetherball, or was that camp one?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can't even remember where camp one was.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know he had put up a tetherball, and we played, I think we had a spot there, we played croquet. Some of the guys would come up, and I remember, I had a picture someplace, one of them, I don't remember his name, but he came up there, he just loved to play tetherball. He and Ed would play that of an evening after they finished their eating. And we just wandered through the woods. Of course, by the time you have to do your washing on the board, and there wasn't too much housekeeping, had to do some cooking. And I guess we went out every single weekend.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh yeah, everybody left camp on the weekend and came out.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We'd go out Friday as soon as they get through working, and come back as late Sunday as we could make it, so it was only the five days, really.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: In the summertime it was real nice, it was just kind of a summer campout is what it was.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But in the winter it got cold.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, it wasn't too bad the way they had to rig big steps as long as you had fire and everything would.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was the camp one where they had the fire moved us out, I guess.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was trying to think of where camp one was.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Where was camp one?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Down in Park Country.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's what I was thinking, that was the first camp. Over the hill.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was the first camp when you got down, that was it. Then afterwards they moved to camp eleven, I think it was. But that first one, we had to go up a hill, you know, and then it was down over the hill, and the fire had started, so they had all the shackers get their things ready and have it at a ready in case they couldn't get the fire to go back. They were backfiring, but the wind was blowing right toward our shacks. So they thought probably that we would have to move, and they finally got so kind of dangerous there that they gathered us all up and took us up over the hill away from it. So we stayed all night in some house down there. All of us, there were quite a few then. We were parked on chairs and oven ports and floors and everything else. Quite a long night. But then the wind changed. They were going to load our cabins out of the flatcars. They had the cars all ready. They would just pick them up and set them on the flatcars to see and move them out if they got too dangerous. And they had them at ready, but then the wind changed, and they backfired and it was no more danger to the shacks, so the next day we could move back in. I remember that was quite an exciting session. And that, I think, was the same time they had that big fire when we came out. We had to drive through smoke, and Ed walked ahead of the car, because the smoke was so thick we couldn't see ahead. So he walked down the road, and I drove and followed him because nobody could see a road.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And that was between, where was that? Between Camp and Bowville or?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't, I wasn't. I was trying to think. I believe I was out on the farm.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Might be.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: See, fall of 31, I ever died, and it was a 31, yeah. And I went out there at 31, 32. Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, fall of 31.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that the year that they had that fire in there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I believe it was. I think so.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Anyway, that was, we sure went through. I stayed there on the farm until 34.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That must have been, where would, that would have been this side of Oldville, maybe. It would have had to been, because we came out at Helmer.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. You went in at Helmer, and across Boulder Creek and back into Park from this side. You could go through and come out on the other end, too, and go into Elk River.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But there wasn't any help. But it seems to me, I can't just remember now where about us it was, but it was after we got on the road on the highway. Oh, there was a metal track in there, and I've been trying to think for a minute. And the snow was so deep in there, we couldn't take the cars in. So when we'd walk out, we'd walk the railroad track out to where we had our cars parked. And then came, and I think that was the year that I bumped in the car, the wheelies. Anyway, it wasn't guiding right. And we'd come traveling along this highway. And of course, the snow was piled up high on both sides of the highway. We couldn't get off, but that stupid car all of a sudden made up its mind and head back for a camp, or else it would go, and all of a sudden it would climb up a stump out the side.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was at Camp 7, then.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that Camp 7?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was Camp 7. Because I remember Ed was telling me about it, and I took and drove it. And it was towing out this way. I straightened it up. Of course, I couldn't get it right, but it was a whole lot better. But that's where you'd go down the road, and all of a sudden you'd turn right square over and go off the road. You never knew it was going to turn to the right or the left. It would have helped if you didn't know in which way it was going to go. But you didn't know. And it just reversed, and here all of a sudden it would turn right around in the middle of the road, and it hit back the other direction. But the wheels were just setting out this way, on it. Angling. The other front. In either direction. They started just towing in just at least a bit. They were towing up. Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I stared to death.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And I said, do we have to go out this weekend?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I just dreaded the trips out because of that stupid car. After we got it lined up, then it was fine. But I think we traveled all that winter where that thing happened like that. I believe you did.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was in the summer when I straightened it up.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I know we walked the railroad track out because we couldn't drive in. The snow was so deep and they kept the railroad track open, but not the roads for the shackers.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, they had a freight road in. They freighted in with horses. They'd come in with horses anyhow, and that was open. That went straight from seven back, from seven I know they did. They had about three miles in the boat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I know that one time that old shea came by, the railroad, on a loaded one, Andy Dyer was driving, or was the engineer. And our road from camp came right down.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Remember that steep hill just right in the railroad track, right at the bottom?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I know I was driving that time too, and we came right down that hill. And there, just got there, and here the old shea came by. And pulled it over to the side, and Andy Dyer was on there. He said, boy, you had me scared there for a minute. I thought we'd got you. We thought you had too.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then we had, there was, that must have been camp one where Joe Parks were in there. And Ed Welch was there. She and I just did a lot of traveling. We even rode on the logs that they'd bring in. And she had a baby.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was,

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: at camp one, then they let us go down. Our shacks were close enough to the camp, so we used their showers, and their washrooms there too. And it seems to me like the kitchen help, they used to give us food. It was the wild too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Used to what?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I say at the kitchen, I think they used to give us something to eat once in a while for a treat. They'd have something more than they needed or something. They'd give it to the women in the camps.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was a real nice camp. I liked camp seven and camp one both. I'd laugh at camp seven when they first went out there. We were just building roads and stuff. And I was sitting at the same table as Hiram Cole. There was Hiram, Oscar, Sturgill, and myself. Clem Harris. There wasn't over about 10, 12 people in the camp. And I don't know, I guess the cook had a great big kettle of soup. He must have had it sitting out in the porch saving it until the next day. Because Hiram, we was all sitting there eating and Hiram ate one bowl of soup. He gets a dipper full and puts it in. He'd reach it down to Chipmunk. He carried it out to the cook and he lays it down.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He said, here, he said, why don't you feed your pets instead of letting them help themselves?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And he kept right on going. The rest of you showed her soup beside me.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was another thing that at one of the camps, I know I had my floor waxed. You could just skate on it just about. And a Chipmunk came in there. They just would come right around the cabin, come in the cabin. And we had a kitten at the time there at this particular one. And the Chipmunk was in there and the kitten came in. And he tried to catch that Chipmunk. And of course, all they did was just stand there and paw on this wax floor. They couldn't either one of them get anywhere. I remember Ed and I just nearly left ourselves silly over watching those two animals. One of them trying to get away and the other one trying to get it. Neither one of them could get anywhere. Finally, I guess the squirrel, maybe it was a squirrel or something. Anyway, he finally managed to make it jump and he got outdoors and escaped. But it was real fun to watch him.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I forget what camp it was in. What camp it was in, I believe it was up out of Clarky. A couple of sires, there was about four feet of snow and they had to dig down. And the stumps had to be, white pine trees had to be cut 16 inches from the ground. And there was a great big white pine tree and they saw as a notch and it gets around the back. Saw it and the tree tipped over and a big black bear jumped out of the stump. But there were four feet of snow and they couldn't get out. The bear, he got out and took off. But every once in a while that winter and the rest of the winter, the bear would find another place to hibernate and then they'd hook on to the logs, get under logs. They'd hook on to the logs to take off and hear the bears go to the rest.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: That's the bear?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Who it was that was sawing there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The bear often came up around the camp because they threw out so much garbage, you know. So practically everyone, I guess, had a bear.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But only that one time that they killed the bear to feed everybody.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, one camp, that was up out of Bovill the other way towards Clarky. The bears used to get out around the garbage dump. And I don't know who it was. They took a five -gallon cake and they drove spikes in it all around and put honey in the bottom of it. Now that was what I'd call cruelty. There was a whole bunch of them sitting around there. The bear comes in there. The bear wouldn't bother the people. But he gets his head down and that and then he couldn't get it out. See the nails and gouges. Oh, the bear went crazy. He would just turn around there in circles and he finally hit it against a tree and busted it. And then everybody left. Yeah. Everybody left then.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He got out then, did he?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, he got it off, yeah. And he was mad. He was taken after anybody that was in sight.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You must have been a bachelor then in the part of that time.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I was a bachelor up until I was 50 years old.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you go out and celebrate with somebody? No, didn't I celebrate?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I ain't telling all them stories. Well, let's see.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How do we say this? The Lumberjacks are noted for their celebrations and fights that go on with it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yep. They work hard in all that weather, cold and wet and everything until they get a spake made.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then they head for Spokane. Have a big weekend. Well, I never went and went to Spokane. You never went to Spokane. It's the only time we went to Spokane that we'd be starting out. If there was no work here, we'd go to Spokane looking for work. We never went to Spokane. But a lot of them did. Oh, yeah. But if one fella went to Spokane, he had to go in. He'd draw all this time. He had to have a hat. He'd come back broke and no hat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And he'd go up and lose everything they had. Come back broke.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I've seen some of them fellas sitting in the logging camps. They'd be patching underwear and everything. They'd go up to the commissary, get anything they needed because they had the money coming, you know, just charge it. But they had sit there and patch clothes that I wouldn't even think about. And they couldn't afford to buy new ones. And when they'd draw their time, go to Spokane and buy clothes, come back and not a stitch. They wouldn't have a stitch of clothes. Just put the hat and start all over again, patching them up.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was this from drinking or gambling or both?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, they'd just drink and gamble. They'd get drunk before they'd get there, and then people there, there was just nothing but living off of them, you know. And they'd just keep them drunk until they were broke. And then they had no money. They couldn't get any credit. Had no money, they'd come back to camp broke. No money. All those two or three fellas did even have the money to pay their income tax. They were going in and paying that and spent it all in heroes' income tax time. Yes, it had only taken them a month to make enough. And they didn't know what they were going to do. So they were talking to me, and I said, Well, the government's got it here. At that time, they'd give you slips. And you'd send in how much you made and put the information out and send it in to them. And they'd figure it and mail it back to you. And as long as they had that by the income tax date, it was all right, March 15th, I think it was at that time.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And so I said, What did you try of that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that's what they did. It worked. By the time they got it back with the old dad, the money said they could pay it. Well, that saved a lot of them. They had given 30 days extra.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We had this little tiny stove to bake on there. But I made gingerbread one night. That must have been about a camp seven. No, that's when we had the log cabins. That must have been camp one. And it made homemade ice cream. It's winter. And it had ice cream and gingerbread, and it did taste good. And then Ed ate so much, he got sick.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, boy, was he sick that night?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Aspirately sick. I was really worried.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I guess you didn't have a doctor very close. No, no doctor. If he just stayed sick the next day, I expect he'd be happy gone. But by the next day, he had recovered reasonably. I think he was probably able to work.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Able to work reminds me of up in the Elk River Basin. Ollie and I were sawn together up there. It was my birthday, the first day of October. He was telling everybody in camp, boy, he was sure going to work for me that day. And I just laughed. I was fine. So the next morning I got up, the people were sitting around laughing. I couldn't figure out what was the matter.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I went down to the restrooms there and come back and hear the underwear hanging on the railing along. They were all dirty. I don't know what some of them got to eat that night. The whole camp, there was only two or three of us in the whole camp that it didn't affect. They all had diarrhea.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then they were sitting in their lab room, and I said, what's the matter?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was one of them. He couldn't hardly eat breakfast. We went to work, but he worked me all right the next day. He'd help me fall up trees, and I'd start sawn, and he'd have to go. He'd come back, make one cut, and he'd have to take off, and I'd keep a sawn and he'd really work me off. But it was a hard way of doing it. But that whole camp was sick. I don't know. They had some kind of a cream codfish or something, and I didn't eat any of it. And I think maybe that's what it was, but a lot of them, they just... I don't know why I didn't. I liked cream codfish, but I didn't eat any that night, and some of them just couched. You know, eating all the... I think that was it. All some of them didn't even make it there to the outhouse.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When did you start working up in the woods?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: In the woods. I think it was 22 when I first...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Uh... Yeah, from 1906 to 1922. I went up there, and I passed as 18. I signed up. You had to be 18 to go to the woods. I signed up as 18, and I went right to work. No troubles.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This was just one of Potlatch's regulations?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That is for Oscar Sturgill.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then the next day I had to tell him how old I was. When Oscar had come out on the job, and he'd sure ask me all the time.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But being 18 years old, you know, I'd sit there and I'd wonder, well, how do you know how old I am?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we would make big money, too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was we getting?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Two and a half a day or something like that, a board, something like that working with a day. That was big money at that time.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Okay.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then they finally raised it to a dollar, oh, four dollars a day, and we'd paying a dollar twenty cents a day for board. So if you didn't have too many rainy days in there, or stormy days, you didn't work. You'd done all right. But then the board would eat up the rest of the money. You didn't work.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Stay in there Saturday and Sunday, that would cost you money, but if you come out, it'd cost you more.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, finally, they didn't do it by the day, they did it by the thousands. This was all gipo then, by the thousands.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I guess some of them still worked by the day. Oh, I don't work by the day. They could gipo, the sawyers gipo in the tasters. Well, the sawyers at that time, I think, was sawn by the thousands. But I was working on landings and cutting roads, you know, just working by the day when I first went in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: When they gipoed by, then they could work as hard, fast as they could, and the harder they worked, the more money they made. So, of course, they wanted to gipo.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And is it Camp 4, Camp 5 north of Boville?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was on one job there, and I was swamppin' for a man. Oh, we had a beautiful place, but I had to peel all the logs.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: They were so big, and he was drivin' on the run, and I asked him, how much we gettin' for this? Two and a half thousand, that's what Dave's gettin' over there, and I said, well, did you ask the boss?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, he said he'd tell us tomorrow.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, I worked three days, and the boss come up the fourth day, and I said, when he come back with the drag, I said, did he say how much we was gettin'?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, he said he'd tell us tomorrow. I said, I'm quittin'. I was losin' about five pounds a day the way I was workin'. I dropped 15 pounds in three days. We was really workin'. And I put a shirt on like this. It was one of them blue Cambrai shirts. And a night, when I dried, I could just stand up straight, sweat. Yeah, that's the way it was. So I quit, went even drew my time. And after they took my board out, I made $51 .00 and some cents a day. Oh, I was wishin' I'd stayed.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Fifty -one dollars a day?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Fifty -one dollars a day, and that was in 1925. I got $153 .00 and some cents for three days' work. They paid us off by the thousand. But this man I was workin' with, he worked the whole month. And he made $160 -some dollars. They cut his wages, but they didn't know what to pay to start with. So that's the way they paid me off. If I'd have worked the whole month, I wouldn't have made very much more money than I did in the year. Oh, my goodness. Oh, he would not. Then he quit. I'd seen him in town afterwards, told him how much I made. He made less than they listed across the other side. We had way the best chance. He was mad then. Oh, that was the best money I'd ever made for a long time, was up there of $51 .00. It figured out just about $51 .00 and 20 cents a day above board.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Philip Asplen's brother Titus was a teamster up there at the same time. They had it by those fellows that drove their teams, were sure proud of their horses. Oh, man. Wow. And they had a right to be. They were beautiful horses.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Those teams were just absolutely in the match. Every night they'd go out in the barn, take care of the horses, and feed them and curry, and then they'd argue which is the best all day Sunday. But I guess it'll go on that tape all right.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I forget. It was up in the Elk River Basin, and it was in the winter.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And they were out there, and one fella had a great big boil right in the back here, you know?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. And it was sore. He couldn't sit down or nothing.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So if he was telling another fella, and the fella says, well, he said, have you busted it? Did you have the core out of it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, no. He said, I can't see it. I can't.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He said, will you do it for me?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And the fella says, yeah. They were out in the barn there, so they get back in the corner where the medicine cabinet was. The man hauls his pants down in this fella. He worked on it. He got it all in the car and everything out. He just reaches up and gets a turn -to -turn bottle, pours on us. Yee -ow! That man took right out of it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He landed right, all there, right out in the snow grip, right?

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Can you tell me there was a scream out of that fella?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: One of the kind of tricks that was pulled...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, that was a trick. He made a mistake. Oh, that would be a mean trick. Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: None of the skinners could figure out what it happened. That old turpentine, that would burn. The fella says, I'll never turpentine another dog.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was pretty good. That's okay.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that reminds me of what you were telling me yesterday about the home remedy that your mother had.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I know what old man Ferguson used. Robert Ferguson, you know, Clarence and his dad.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This, what do you call it, asafoetty or something?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He'd put that in an old dirty sock and he'd wear that around his neck all the time when they flew or anything was going around. That's what he wore. The cold was going around. He had it in a dirtier sock, the better it was.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He had that all the time. Then he carried a potato in his back pocket for rheumatous. Then he'd have a copper band on his hand, around his wrist. I've seen a lot of them of that. But he carried a potato in his back pocket for rheumatous and I don't know where he figured that would help.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did it help?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't know. I don't know how bad he had rheumatous or anything, whether it eased up or not. But that was one of his home remedies for that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I wanted to get on tape the one that your mother used about the...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: All that... Cut on your leg. We didn't... Well, that was just bread dough. I've made it out of bottom bread, anything. Just milk. Just make it just so it's a dough, you know, not too thick.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: This is after cook, so...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It's regular bread, yeah. Just take a loaf of bread, just take a little of the center out of it and put milk. Now, of course, there in the camps we had nothing but tannins milk. I'd put that in, but Mother just used regular cow's milk and homemade bread. But just so it's soft, not too soft, just put that right over it and put a rag around it, bandage and tie it there. The next morning it'll all be drawn right out. It's a polis, is what it is. It just draws it out.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But what does it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't know. But it sure works. I know when she took that off my leg the next morning, it was this sliver sticking out about that far. She got a hold of it. It was a good inch long. She got a hold of it and pulled it right out. I put some more back on for a day, I think, to draw all the passing stuff out of there because it was infected. Cleaned up, never bothered again.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And you've done this since?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I forget. It was one... I forget what I had on my thumb, but it was a great big swelling. It just took it all out overnight, and there was just a big hole underneath. It drew all the parts and everything right out of it. Oh, it works. It's good. For just something like that. It didn't want to cure all for everything.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Put it on your toothache too, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Was there anything special she did for toothaches or...

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah, toothache.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I died. We get toothaches. We couldn't get to the dentist.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Mother takes iodine and put it in. That'll kill your toothache. But then us kids, if she wasn't there, we'd get it, you know. We'd take a toothpick with cotton. I think it was iodine, and we'd fill it on there. And we'd have great big sores, you know, that burn.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, what was it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It seemed to me like it... No, carbolyc acid.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Mervin. Now, that'll kill the nerve in a tooth. That'd kill lots of things. We'd have great big sores, let our little mother get it in there, just take a little and put it in there. We had to be awful careful. She'd hold it in there for a while. And then maybe take another one with clean cotton and wash it off. But we as kids, since she wouldn't be home and we'd have a toothache, get a gob out and put it in there, quit aching, pull it out. Maybe sometimes we'd just take the toothpick out of the cotton and clamp down on it. Oh, we'd be so awful looking sight. Oh.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You were saying something about a beet pullus? How'd they make that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Grated beets and put it on a cloth.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Put it over a sore.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And wrap it up.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Made it all there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I guess it did.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And the mother used hammermet, wasn't it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: When we'd have toothache or something, she'd put a little on the cotton and put the peppermint, oil a peppermint, oil a wintergreen, either one, and tuck that into an aching tooth.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: A lot of times we had a cavity, take clothes, and put the clothes. Boil a clothes, too. We'd take the whole cloth and be on the long stem and we'd put that in and it would hold it there. Oh, we kind of tickled and we'd get that. That was kind of fun to hold a bat in.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We never did that, but we seemed like we had bottles with a little liquid in them. That's what we used. Oil a clothes, oil a peppermint, oil a wintergreen, something like that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Tucked it in for... And mother crows.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What's mother crows?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That was for diarrhea.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's something liquid, or... We'd take that. Mother's clothes remedy. Ah, okay.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's what I gave one of my little squirrels. I gave caskroil to one of them, mother crows to one of them. One of them died and only if I don't know which one will switch. I'm surprised that either one did. They both had diarrhea.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do either of you remember your parents telling stories? What did they come from?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: England or...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did they have old fashioned stories that they used to tell?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, born in this country, one of my grandfathers on mother's side, my grandfather on mother's side was born in England. Or part of the family was born in England. I think maybe he was born in York State. But when my mother's mother was born in St. Lawrence, those islands in the St. Lawrence River, they were originally, definitely, from England. In fact, we have worked out a family tree and I do have somebody that signed the charter on the Mayflower. Oh, look at this. So we are definitely from the isle.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You're the original, sir.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did your mother have any tales from Norway that she didn't?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't remember any of them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: My dad, old dad, had a lot of stories to tell, but I don't remember. He used to sing a lot of little songs in Sweden and he'd translate them in English, but I don't remember.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: None of my folks came over in the Mayflower. I had laughed to one fellow. He was telling me, but this folks came over in the Mayflower and he was bragging, really wasn't bragging about it. He was telling him, he said, well, I don't know whether I should brag about it or not.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And I said, why?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He said, for the simple reason, the damn cuss was run out of England. Yeah. But he got over this country. They were all right. They were run out of England all of that. Yeah. And Elitist is there. Well, I don't know.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The one that's there, they might have run him out of England.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They ran a lot of them out. And they were good people. It was just that they didn't believe what they were doing over there. And that's the reason they got out. Some of them had run out. Some of them got out before they got caught up. Yeah. But there was nothing wrong with the people.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you know why your parents came over?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I don't know. I know my mother. I think there were two of them. He had a sister who came over. And dad and two brothers came knowing. I guess his brothers come first and he come afterwards. He came.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But why?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They, well, I imagine just to get away from over there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: So they met and buried in a very long time.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's how you got a Swedish father. Oh, I don't know. Maybe my dad was run out of Sweden. I think he was. You never know.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What's that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I said, maybe dad was run out of Sweden. Maybe that's the reason he got out of Sweden. I don't know. He wouldn't have told the lad if he had. Oh, I think he would have. He probably would have. Yeah, there was nothing wrong with that. He could tell some good ones. He always had a crowd around him on Sundays when he lived out there in the ridge. All the young fellas that come over there. They'd have a bunch of beer and they'd sit around and they all come over to see Martin and have your stories.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember any of them?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I don't. Well, I wouldn't be around. I was out working as a farmer kid, so.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I remember that time that your brother Bill, wasn't it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Dr. W. H. He was out on the field.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Bill, it was some different cheese. Took some Limburger cheese.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Inside of his hat band. On the inside, his father's hat band. He hated it. He hated Limburger cheese. Anyway, with a passion, you know what it smells like. His son -in -law, Hilda's husband, at that time was Limburger -themed. He ate Limburger cheese all the time. So, Melvin's father was out in the field, out there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He came in and, tell me now, you say, what did he say?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He came in and he was cussin'. I don't know any damn place I go, I smell Limburger cheese. One of the hot, blacked -up woods teeth. The rest of them forgot about Bill, but they did. Oh, you talk about man being mad for a while. The rest of us all knew about it, so we were just, that could be dying.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You'll be giving these kids some ideas.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Before they get called in, who's not here?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I think it's not going to be. Yeah. And waiting for some more good stories.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And waiting for some more good stories.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I don't know, I just can't think.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Are there any other characters?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I can tell you.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was talking to Harold Milton down here this morning, you know. He was asking about the games we played and what we did at literary.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did he take?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That. They had the literaries of Victor Berg and two other fellows. They'd put on just short skips, you know. And there's one fellow who's completely bald -headed. And he'd go ahead and he'd glue a little piece of sandpaper onto the back of his head. They'd be pulling all kinds of crazy stuff, so I'm going to reach up and strike a match on it. This batch of pop sparks fly all over. The sandpaper is just about the color of his head. You couldn't make out to start with a lot.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But that was really a sight for you to get to see it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was a ton of names for the other two too, and I can't believe it. The thing he used to do quite a bit of was put these plates on. Even Frank Rocky and my husband, my two, we had the old, where the old Odd Fells Hall was down here. That's been good many years ago too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Put on plates.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that is right. You know where the Lutheran Church is. Right across Side Street. The Odd Fells Hall stood right there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Where the apartment was?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Where the old hall was there. That was the Odd Fells Hall.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we used to have dances there in the wintertime. One winter there was nothing going on, so King Walker and myself, we started, we just called up everybody in the country and told them we were going to have a dance. And we had to pay Doc Parr and Jewels, by the way. Told them it would be 50 cents a nation. Well, we had a big crowd. We had enough to pay Doc and pay for the rent of the hall. That's all we cared for. Next week somebody else took it, and we had dances going all winter.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We used to do all kinds of things in there. They used to have big parties in there. Oh, yeah. Not only dances, but they'd have puppies, too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I was thinking, when did the telephone come in? When did the telephone service come in?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, we had telephones.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We must have had telephones by 1915 or so, up there on the farm.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I think so. It was a long time ago. I remember when the first radio, I ever heard the first radio, was the year I taught over at Union School.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What year was that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: One of the young fellows there had made a crystal set, and all of us were so excited about that radio, and invited us down to their house so we could hear the radio. And there we all sat with our ears glued to it, and, oh, isn't it wonderful, but we didn't hear much.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The first radio that I heard, we'd went to Moscow for a Fourth of July celebration, and we drove over there with one of them old hacks. So that was before 1920, it was in probably 18. I was just a kid. But they had a big street dance. And this radio, they had it up on the side, and that's where they got their music. And I know none of us believed it. We figured it was all a fake that it was a phone drive, but it was a radio.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What year was that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: To be 18 or 19. I think it was 18.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, it was... It might have been 17.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, that would have been... that was a radio.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, they weren't very common, I know, because this kid had made this one, and that was my first experience with it, the radio.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But I know Walt Smith then, the first radio I really heard in my house. You don't make a whole deal out of those now. That's all you need. That was before 1920. I know we'd go over there and sit in the evening, we'd get...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was it San Francisco or some station down there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We'd all sit around there and wait for them to play in the strawberry room. Oh! That piece. That would have come with all ears. That was your favorite. That was our favorite piece.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We used to have an old phonograph that we had in our cabin in the days when they had to turn the crank. Ed was so proud of that phonograph. But before that, you had one that you...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have to keep it turning to keep it playing?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, I was just going to speak about that. We had an old Victor phonograph. And it was a Victrola, but at that time it was a Victor. And it was about that square, the base of it. And it had a turntable on it, and a great big longhorn. Well, Mrs. Bim lived up above there, and the spring broke in it. So you couldn't wind it up, you'd wind it up and it would play a record or two records, maybe a record and a half. Well, she was bound and determined she was going to hear this music. Hear them records, they'd never heard any of them. So mothers, she had us, kids. We'd have to sit and turn the crank, you know, and make it go. Oh, it was beautiful. We wanted to get out of it. And we'd turn it faster a little bit, and turn it slow. That was the music you ever heard. But we didn't get out of it, we had to sit there. Oh, that was beautiful. We never got out of it, we had to cry. Too bad you haven't got that old phonograph right now. The one, the Victrola that led opened up. Them things were worth money. Yeah, I know. What a pity that we let that one go.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: It was, it only played 12 -inch records, however, 78.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, 78, you could play any size, you know, anywhere, 8, 10, 12. You had to set the thing anyhow.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But you couldn't, not for the slow speed, it just had one speed. It had one speed. Well, I've forgotten, I guess we sold it to somebody rather. Meanwhile, of course, we got a three -speed affair, electric. This one you had to crank it up and get it to go, and it would play one record, and then you'd have to crank it up and play another record. But it was a nice one, and Ed was sure proud of that photograph.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When you lived up at the camps, did the fellas kind of come in and mingle with the married guys?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Right.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have the single fellas from the camp in for parties?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Men were all good. They wouldn't go unless they were invited or something. They'd walk by and maybe stop and say hello. But they never bothered otherwise. Oh, no.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There were some of them that were special friends of the fellas, and we'd have them in, what, so high and old for a meal. Or, like I said, we had the games, and they'd come up there and play games. Not too many of them. There were two or three there, especially. Mainly, we hobnobbed mainly with the married, other married people. But there were some of these that Ed worked with that he had in. He said, I'd like to have, I can't remember that one name, that one especially, that was so crazy about Tetherwall. He'd come up there every time he'd get a chance, and he and Ed would have a game of Tetherwall. And he sure did like that. I'd forgotten his name. But when he'd come up, we'd probably have him there. Or we'd have lunch afterward or something, you know. There weren't any rules against the fellas. Oh, no.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, we used to enjoy going down to the barn and watch Whitey.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah. She was a horse. I was trying to think of which one of the fellas it was. Well, this was quite a while before them. It was up north of Bobo. He was the barn boss. The barn boss, he was the barn boss. You done as he said. And there was a law, no smoking in the barn. But one day, he was out there doing work. Here's a man all dressed up. A couple of them, you know, just one of them all dressed up with a big cigar in his mouth. Starts into the barn. The fella jumped up in front of him, and, hey, he says, you can't go in that barn to smoke. The fella kept running on the go, and he says, you can't go in that barn to smoke.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: The fella said, you know who I am?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He said, I'm AWL -er. That was the head of the whole works. The big boss. You know, the whole work. The man says, I don't give a damn if you're Jesus H. Christ. You're not going in that barn to smoke. The man just looked at him, turned around, and went to lay the cigar out there in the stump. He turned around from there, and he says, that's right. Do it all up. If he'd went in, he might have fired the fella. The fella did face that fella tall, little Mickey. And he didn't let him go in there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you ever run in the lair out there? Did you ever run in the lair?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. He used to come out. I didn't know him because he was way above our class. He'd never come and mingle with the crew at all. We used to see him. He'd come out to the camp. Not too often, but every once in a while. I'm AWL -er.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was he driven around in his car? Did he come out there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, this was before they were driving cars, much as he'd come in on the railroad.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They didn't drive any cars into them camps to speak of before. Well, a lot of the roads, you weren't able to get in until 25, 26, some of the camps. They could in the summertime. It depended on the time of the year.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What were all the different jobs you'd done?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know you mentioned two or three.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I'd done just about everything there was. Island brush, that was one of my first jobs when I was first with the Hindu ones. Then the sauna logs and swamping, drove tea, wheeled roads.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Whoa, whoa, what swampy?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's lemon and getting the logs ready for skidding to the landing. And then dogging.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What's dogging? All right, what's dogging?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, on the smaller logs, we had what we called dogs. They'd be chains about that long with a hook. They'd come in, and you'd just take and you'd drive. There's one hook into the log in front, and one into the log back, but it'd be so. They'd be about that far behind, and they'd hook on front and away they'd go. The other logs behind it you had tacked on and followed.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, okay.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And then I helped build a chute, took the road logs in the chute. I never agreed chute. Now you want to know what chute is. Yeah. Well, that's up there at the Elk River Country, where it's real steep. They'd take and they'd lay one log flat, not too big a one. Then on the sides, they'd lay, they'd cut it out so that it was just a trough. And all that would be steep hills, well, on the top, they'd pull the logs in. They couldn't skid down these because it was too fast for the teams. And then you'd have a landing there to pull them, and they'd roll them into the chute. They'd take off down the chute. They'd just travel over their own tower. They'd just run for themselves, and when they hit the bottom, they'd chute right straight out. And some places it'd be pretty flat. They'd have a man with grease, axle grease, you'd have to grease him. And other places on the hill where it was too steep, they'd get to go on too fast. They'd have what they call a gooseneck. There'd be a square piece of iron about that square, or maybe about that long. That'd come down this way, then over, and then it'd go down into the chute fastened. And the logs would hit that, not to slow them up. But the bark, you'd just see streaks of bark flying through the air. That would take us to be around when that stuff was gone.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you hit water at the end, or ground? No, just hit the ground, doesn't it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, if some places under the rivers, they run right into the rivers. And then the flumes down there, I never worked on the flumes, but down on the camp eggs, through there they had flumes. Well, that was made similar to a chute. Only the water was running in them, the logs would float down. You've seen pictures of them, I think, on TV. That might be. Walt Disney had a show that showed some of that on one time.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Now what did this man with the axle grease have to do?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He'd have to grease the chute. So he'd come down and maybe be a level spot, and then take off again. And he'd watch the logs, and if the logs were slowing up, he'd get in there, he'd have a long swab, a rag, on a pole, and he'd grease that with axle grease. And make it slick. One log would maybe stop there, and the other one would come down pretty fast, hit it, start it, and away they'd go again. Yeah, I bet that must have been a cruel little few accidents up there.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Some of those hills were steep like that, the horses would just go down on the gout to keep from out of the way of the logs. They tried to have the logs kind of come down out of the soil. Well, they always had them hooked on, so that on the hills, and they'd start.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The minute the skinner'd see the logs start to run, he'd just holler, J. And the horses, the first hole there was for them, they'd take right off to the side, and the logs would go on by. The double trees would come unhooked. But them horses knew where it would work. You'd say J, and that meant to get off. It wouldn't mean maybe to be the right -hand side, but they knew all the J -holes, and they'd take off to the side.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Trained horses.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, let's see. Now, you helped build the chutes.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I helped build it, yeah. You had a load of the logs, but you didn't grease. I didn't do any greasing, I don't know. No, I rolled the logs into them. Oh, I do not. I didn't crash. I even worked in the blacksmith shop helping the blacksmiths in between times. The partner laid off or something, we saw him, and then asked the boss if he had anything else. They'd go work in the blacksmith shop, hang axe handles or something. That was a job I did in my axe handles. I didn't, you know, they'd break a lot of handles. Oh. Well, you made a lot of roads, too. Oh, yeah, we built lots of roads, but we talked about that yesterday, the number two shovels.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But helped build a drease in there that was what they hauled the logs in the wintertime.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Where?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: On the left, they said I could pick them.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Pick them?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'll get you an ante to take them.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Let's see, number two shovel is a hand. Yeah, a short handle.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: They've got the long handle spades, but the number twos, we always figured down the short handle. I mean, they were just a spade, you know. We'd keep them sharp as erasure. And we'd just stand, drive down, get a shovel full throat just that way all day long. The only time I'd get a break was on a fire. We'd get, first thing we'd get on a fire, I'd get me a shovel. That's what I wanted. And of course, they'd have fellas with a axis and stuff, clearing the brush out, getting ready, and then there'd be a bunch coming along, digging fire trench. Oh, they'd make trench about that wide.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Is that all? Just about three feet?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that's all. If you tried to make it any wider, they wouldn't get anywhere. Oh, yeah. And if it wasn't burning fast, well, that would hold it, so we could stop it on that. But I'd go to the office and get me a good pile, and I'd get me a shovel. I'd work till I'd get pretty near to the head of the string. Then I'd sit down and start filing my shovel.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Somebody'd say, what do you do?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'm filing my shovel.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Does that help?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, try it. They'd take it and make it cut.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, sharpen mine, will you?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, you take mine and I'll sharpen yours. I'll get that sharpened and somebody else come along. Same thing over, and I'd sit there till the whole string went by. Well, then I'd get my shovel sharp and go to the head of them again, and say if they were over, I'd ever shovel the shovel of dirt.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: The boss would come along, and, what do you do?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I'm sharpening these shovels.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And he'd say, well, that helps, don't it?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, he knew, you know. I'd say, yeah, I know. Boys come along and they want their sharpen, so I'd use trades with them. That's all right. I'd say, keep my people.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, there's tricks in all trades. Now, you better tell your trick about milking cows, too.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: About what?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Milking cows.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that was a long time back. That was back in the twenties.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You mean when I worked for that dairy farmer?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was one spring there was nothing to do around here. The logging camps hadn't over. So I got tired of just laying around and thought, well, I'll go to Moscow. And I'll go to the service station where the farmers should pull in and see if I can get a job. So I went there and I spent some time there. Nobody needed help. So I goes to pull them. I got over there and I pulled into the service station. I asked, and yeah, they said, there's a farmer out here. A dairy farmer needs a man. So they told me where it was and I went out and you bet. He said, I've been looking for a man. So the first job, it was on Monday, the first job I got was hauling cowmen, or four horses in a minersch press. Well, that's a pretty smelly job, but nothing else to do. So I did. I spent a whole week on that. I got done early, on Saturday. And he had a big dairy farm and the college boys would come down, you know, he'd get them to help. And he'd tell me, he'd say, you know, I get these college boys down, and there ain't one of them that know how to milk. But he said, I show them, and he said, they're good milkers. They didn't like it, but they're all good milkers. He said, I just show them how to do it. So this Saturday afternoon, it was real early, so I unhooked the horses and the barn, and I went in and told them I was done. And he said, well, he said, go ahead and turn the horses out in the pasture. So I went and done that and come back in.
