I said, well, since you got anything else for me to do, I figured I was going to get to go home early.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Well, he says, can you milk?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I said, oh, yeah, I'm a good milker. You bet. Oh, fine. He said, milk that cow over there as your real gentle cow. Well, to milk the cows, I'd have to go and wash my hands and clean all of, you know. I didn't do any of this. I just went over and I grabbed a bucket and I sat down at that stool and I started milking. I squirt milk in my hands. I started milking the milk of the running off of my hands down into the bucket. When he wasn't looking, I reached down and got a little straw in through in the bucket. I forgot the cow milk, and he was watching me, but he didn't see me get the straw, of course. But when I got done, I walked over to him and I had the bucket low enough so he could see milk all over the outside of the bucket, running off of my hands. My hands were dirty, you know, running into the bucket.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I said, well, what cow next?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, he said, you've been working all day. He said, you won't have to milk anymore. You can call it a day if you want to. He never bothered me about milking cows, actually. I guess he figured if I was a good milker, why, you know, you tried to teach me. I worked there two weeks after that and he never asked me to milk.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: But you didn't know how to milk, right?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: If I told him I didn't know how, then he would have showed me. But when I told him I was a good milker, I guess he'd give up. Well, I'll tell you, my hands, the milk, we're just running off from them. You're not supposed to do that at all. You're supposed to get rag and warm water and wash them off, brush all the straw and clean them up, you know. And I didn't do nothing with that. I ever went in the bucket. That's when you did all the manure spreaders, too. There was one bucket of milk he didn't sell.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I couldn't even feed it to the pigs. I was wondering, you probably strained it, did sell it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, he didn't dare. See, that milk was spoiled fast.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And he didn't. He wouldn't dare. He dumped that.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's the reason they have to be super -ticular with that milk, if they get anything like that in it, it'll spoil. It'll spoil in a hurry. Like your bread.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was listening to that last night, and I was trying to figure out what you could have done with that bread.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: To what?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: To the bread that you baked.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, the bread I baked. I don't know either. But I know Ed said he threw some of it out to the chickens, and the chickens broke the bills trying to eat it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And the first batches of sours, they curdled the milk, but we still have milk toast.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: But it looked good.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You never told about making the cherry pie in the camp, or wherever you was baking or cooking, too.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: You told them that they didn't like it. That is up in Alaska.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Or is that?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No. Put me to cook enough there. Took the cat skinner and run him in the kitchen for a week. Waiting table and washing dishes. I figured it was enough for me to cook. That is all right. I've made it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, but that still isn't about the pie you told them if they didn't like it, they can make their own. Well, it's worth wanting to keep. Like a guy started to eat his, and he said, well, this is pretty sad, but that's just the way I like it. Well, that is up there. That is up there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was that up there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: First man to kick was to take over. Oh. And I couldn't get anybody to kick. But they'd done a three -weeks job in two weeks. So I don't know whether my cooking was so good to add so much energy or if it was so far that they wanted to get back home. But I had a mate. Nobody in all days, you know, no dinner to get. I'd send out lunch. And then I'd just jump on the cap and ride over to the other camp. It was about two and a half, three miles over the hill. I'd be over there at dinner time, and I'd eat, and I could live on one wheel a day.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did the cooks and the old lumber pants ever pull any practical jokes?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, I don't remember any, really.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: There was an awful difference in the cooks. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: My.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Some of them could cook and some they hated.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How about Marsh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He was Billy Marsh.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was he a good cook?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, he was a good cook.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: We had a good cook up there at Camp 5 north of Bovill. Ralph Hanson. He was just a young fella. And he was good. He wasn't, you know, he wasn't the best, but he was good. Everybody bitched and complained. Everything was wrong. So finally they transferred in, put him some other place and sent another one in. Then they had reasons to cook. They didn't get nothing. But I always found that the ones that done the most, kick him, was the ones that never had anything to eat at home. They never had anything at home. People that did have something good at home, they knew what was good and what wasn't.

He'd always get up early and go out, feed them, hurry them, harness them. And he got out. Here's horses were all harnessed. And he looked at him and looked at him. Then he fed him. And he went in for breakfast and he told his wife, he said, I don't know what's happened to me. He said, I forgot to unharness my horses. If worried, you don't. You went to go to work, brings them out. And of course, they didn't have the right harness and the right horses. The horses that had worked on the right -hand side, they'd maybe have the left -hand harness on them. Or one that was in the center, they'd have some of the outside harness on them. And everybody would meet at a certain place, and all just pull in on this one and batter down the place, practically, with our noisemakers. And we'd usually do it quite soon after a couple was home, after they had been married. We'd come to their house, and then they would drag out the treats, and we'd have a nice social evening. So when Ed and I were married, that's when I guess I think it kind of almost backfired, but anyway, we just took for granted. We were both old -timers around the, you know, not old, but then we were here and had lots of friends each, so we just knew they'd all bragged about what they was going to do to us when we were married. We figured we'd have these other kind of treats, like oranges and candy and food like that. And we waited week after week after week went by, and of course, oranges will only keep so long, so we gave them away and ached on. The oranges were gone, and as people would come and the family would come while we ate candy, and the candy was gone, and I think we did have cookies or something, and they only last so long, too, so we ate those. And I don't remember just exactly how long it was, and we just decided, and we felt a little bit let down because we had expected to be thoroughly chivalry, naturally. And they never did come, and we wondered why. They never came, and she was. Well, one night, it got late, and we'd gone to bed, and all of a sudden, the racket began. Ed and I both said, and nothing on hand, and of course, the stores were all closed, no chance to get anything, and here it was. So when they came in, we just... So, it was just too bad.

They didn't have molds like this. Oh, yeah. We hadn't been here very long and Mother got one of these butter molds.

words: What were some of your jobs while you were working at the cream? What were some of your duties while you were working at the cream rate? Some of the things that we did, the duties there?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, that was when the cream would come in, it would come in in various kinds of... No one brought the same kind of a container, they brought small containers, gallons or five gallons or ten gallons. We'd stir it all up and then we had a special dipper with a little spout out of our little lip on it, or whatever you'd call it. We'd stir it all up and take out enough to put in our little glass container. And when we'd get enough to fill a thing that, whatever that was, that you'd put it in there, and then after you get them in there, then you'd turn it on, oh yeah, we had to add a little acid of some kind in each one. And then you'd turn it on, it'd go whipping around straight out until a certain time. And then after that had done right, then you'd measure it, see how much cream butter fat there was in it, and you'd mark that on the card. Each man came in or whoever came in had their name, and you'd put on there, you'd weigh it, you'd have to weigh it when they brought it in. And then after you'd determine how much butter fat was in it, you'd put that down, because they got paid by the butter fat.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Then we saw that smoke coming, and Mother and I walked up to the top of the hill to see if we could, because at that time of the day, in the month and the year, that was a bad time to have many fires. And we walked up there to see if we could see where it was. It wasn't a very big one at that time. And then Ed was really anxious to get back to camp, because we had quite a ways to go. It was getting late, so we left. And then practically you might say, oh hell, pop loose after we left, because then it just all of a sudden it was a raging inferno.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: And I said, after a minute, I missed all the excitement. The wind came up all at once, and I fired him up for a mile, mile and a half a stretch. They'd think they'd have it out here in a mile or two farther on. There was fire coming up. They carried the sparks, chunks of bark and old burning stuff.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I know Hester was home, I guess. Anyway, all of them, they just worked furiously to save the place, because it was right at their door. I know they had gone and they were evacuating people and telling them to go to town. They set up places downtown where they could take them in and fix beds and food for them. The Old Rift Tarnes place weren't down. That's right, it did. The barn and everything. That's the fellow that had owned your place originally. That was the one that we bought the place from him.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He just lived, what would it be, a third of a mile north of us?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: A quarter of a mile. A third of a mile.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Between around a third of a mile.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He was kind of a character, wasn't he?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Oh, he was. He used to have an old screech owl.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Have you heard about him?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Well, Helena told me a couple of little things.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Are you going to tell some secret stories? Did you tell her about the screech owl he had? Did you tell her about the screech owl he had?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I told her so, but you go ahead and see what you see. But this old screech owl, and he called it Pete. Everything he had was Pete. Oh. And he'd get out there and cook Pete, cook Pete. And here the owl would come and land on his shoulder. One night we'd gone home from, we'd been into the show and it was dark. We were just kids. We'd go on by there and I started hollering, cook Pete.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Didn't that three or four times that I got the light scared of me?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He'd come and land it right off his shoulder.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And Bert Rudder, did you tell her about, tell her?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: No, I didn't tell him about that one. Oh, Bert Rudder, he was down there. He'd stay with Griff quite a bit. And Bertie was going down after water one morning. He was going by there and he stuck his, Griff stuck his head out of the door.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And Bert, Bert, what do you want?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: If you want a mushroom breakfast you've got to come and pick it first. The mice had got into it.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: That's what he was saying.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And Pete would sleep, huh?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: I was telling her too about that screech owl, how we took, we'd catch mice at home and take them up there and how he'd eat them and swallow them down. Then after a while he'd spit the skins and the feet out. And he used to screech, you know, you'd call him. Then he'd let off the damn discreetch.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He had horses, you know, he's quite a horseman. And he went to break one horse, it was a three -year -old, three or four -year -old. And the horse was stubborn, he couldn't make a move.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Why don't we make a move?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: He goes and gets another team, puts a chain around the horse's neck, hooks that on the front, takes off and broke the horse's neck and kills it. Oh!

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: The horse was no good to him the way he was. It wasn't moving.

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Yeah, that fella was a character out of it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you know him?

speaker: Melvin Carlson or Helena Carlson
words: Burt Rudder.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was he the one, was that Burt Rudder?
